When such a recognition of meaningful boundaries is torn apart and thrown into oblivion, much is at risk. Home and Feeling-at-Home as Theoretical Premise The pressure toward constant transformation which is everywhere today, is intimately related to a sense of exile and homelessness which is also found at 4 Weber discussed this concept in several places, most explicitly in Chapter IV of Economy and Society, entitled Household, Enterprise and Oikos (Weber 1978: 370–84); perhaps too reductively, he saw the oikos as ‘organized want satisfaction’ (ibid.: 381). Liminality and the Modern 226 the level of thought and philosophy. Any effective response to this development must entail a reconsideration of human experiences of liminality and how such experiences can and must be channelled back into a feeling-at-home in the world. It is impossible simply to feel at home everywhere if one does not feel at home somewhere. The recognition of the liminal characteristics of modernity was certainly what brought Szakolczai in the ‘opposite’ direction, leading him to introduce the concept of ‘home’ as central to social and political theory (Szakolczai 2008). It does in fact seem that there is no other remedy to permanent liminality than to re-establish some kind of background in which individual action can be understood and measured, and in which frenetic movement finds a rest. More than ever, we need to turn to the concreteness of lived space. This discussion takes us right back to the attempts by Victor Turner to align his study of ritual passages with an experientially-based philosophy, via Dilthey. What Dilthey objected to was not only the ‘objectifying’ tendencies of modern philosophy, but also something existing at a deeper level, namely the positing of an external position from where the thinker could engage with and order the universe. We have already argued that such an external position was what unified the world- views of Descartes and Hobbes, and was brought to its climax in Kant. Dilthey argued that such an external perspective, reminiscent of the natural sciences, would only create a splintered image of the human being (Szakolczai 2011). Dilthey’s philosophy of life instead takes as its starting point life itself as it comes to consciousness in the individual in the act of living experience (Erlebnis). However, to Dilthey, as to Kierkegaard, this living experience can never be a solipsistic experience. The self is always embedded in transpersonal relations with objects and other subjects. Dilthey would always insist on the concreteness of these relations. The individual, in the midst of these relations, tries to cognize the world in which he lives with a Weltbild. As Turner understood perfectly well, such a Weltbild is not just a cognitive phenomenon: it draws on feeling, willing and thinking, on the various conative, cognitive and affective dimensions, in proportions that vary, but whose bringing together in lived reality has clearly aesthetic qualities. Once again, this was in fact something that Arnold van Gennep had more or less taken for granted. To him, the study of society meant ‘to show the interaction of the feelings, concepts and judgments that constitute individual psychic activity and collective psychic activity within a given social group’ (as quoted in Belmont 1979: 130). As he jotted down in his unpublished notes, any custom ‘is the expression of a psychic, rational, emotional and social complex’ (ibid.: 131). And to van Gennep, no custom existed on its own: it was organically linked to all others. The notion of Weltbild and Dilthey’s attempt to get to the core of experience is therefore also part of Dilthey’s effort to overcome the dualism between object and subject. He wanted to ground a philosophy which could deal with life as lived by real human beings, one that is therefore also concerned with wholeness, not fragmentary, disconnected individual experience. Cartesian self-introspection By Way of Conclusion: Out of Liminality 227 would in and by itself lead nowhere. In Szakolczai’s reconstruction, Dilthey’s great intuition, which he never managed to fully substantiate, was that: this implies starting in the middle, instead of dividing and separating everything from the start into irreconcilable dualisms and dichotomies. Erlebnis, Weltanschauung and Weltbild each belong to this in-between, at the same time foundational and formative space. Weltanschauung and Weltbild, in particular, are aspects of those conceptual tools by which human beings try to make sense of their experiences, interpreting the events around them, giving and discovering meaning in their lives. (Szakolczai 2011: 107) Following this line of reasoning also means facing two alternative starting points for living and thinking: either being attuned to the world or being split from it, either living the world as a cosmos or living the world as a chaos, either reasoning from within the bounds of the world and its inbuilt rhythms, or forcing an accommodation of oneself to an external, alien reality. A Sort of Homecoming: On Being-at-Home in a Meaningful World We live in this world. There is no other world, and hence no possibility of withdrawal. Any attempts to ‘take us out of modernity’ are tragically part of the modern project. As Foucault spelled out very clearly in his last writing (Foucault 1984), this was precisely Kant’s definition of the Enlightenment: an exit. Any position of exteriority is futile. Any imagined position of a-posteriority is merely tragic, part of the utopian drive that gave the modern project much of its destructive energy. Such positions are not far away from the indeed untenable positions of scientific objectivity that dominated most of the twentieth century. They are all predicated upon a sense of alienation, a setting oneself against or above given conditions; they imply the loss of that balance between ‘involvement and detachment’ (Elias) which is a pre-condition of effective thought and practice. This book has attempted to open a debate on how we think and live with liminality
